subject: Astronomy & Space
title: Antimatter Asymmetry
description: Learn Antimatter Asymmetry in one minute.

hook.text: The Big Bang should have made equal amounts of matter and antimatter. They'd annihilate, leaving nothing. Yet here we are.

buildup.text: For every billion antimatter particles, the Big Bang made one billion and one matter particles. That tiny excess is all we see.

discovery.text: CP violation—where matter and antimatter behave slightly differently—was found in 1964. But it's not enough to explain the gap.

twist.text: The full explanation is unknown. New physics beyond the Standard Model is needed to account for our existence.

climax.text: Experiments at CERN and neutrino detectors worldwide are searching for the missing piece. The answer may rewrite particle physics.

punchline.text: One extra particle per billion. That tiny imbalance built every star, planet, and person.

quiz.question: What is the antimatter asymmetry problem?
options: The Big Bang should have made equal matter and antimatter, yet the universe is almost entirely matter
Antimatter is more abundant than matter but can't be detected by telescopes
Dark matter is actually antimatter that has been hiding in the cosmic void
